opp.ais_why:
• Buyer behaviour is sliding strongly. The next-step note records that Starbucks Sourcing and Procurement is moving to Nashville and disrupting the team, with the seller noting "Per news post on 3rd March...This is going to slide". The Nashville move is still causing slower response and the rep is asking whether the deal is going to close. The RFI focuses on retail, POS, QSR fitment and Oracle on-prem integration, not AI.
• The buyer's stated requirements per the description are retail-related questions on merchandise ordering and gross margin/discounts, reference accounts in coffee shop or QSR, and Oracle on-premise integrations. There is zero buyer-stated mention of Merlin, Agentic, ANA, Intake or GenAI. Products include Merlin, Merlin Intake and Agentic AI, but all of that is Zycus-pushed. The seller notes the RFI is being treated more like an RFI than an RFP.
• Avoma was fully exhausted - zero calls at opportunity, account or starbucks.com domain across a multi-month RFI cycle, which is extremely unusual for a $500K deal and suggests Ariba-managed dialogue. Given the slide, the Nashville disruption and zero AI-specific buyer asks across the detailed RFI, this is Resistant rather than just cool.

metrics.narrative: No quantified business case or ROI has been articulated by the buyer across any available evidence. The RFI scope (per Next_Step__c Dec 2025–Jan 2026) included 'Retail related questions for Merchandise ordering and Gross Margin / Discounts' and 'limited merchandise in eProc / Ordering and pass through data for QOH, Alternate Products, and possibly non-food merchandising', signaling process automation and retail-merchandise integration as use cases, but no cost savings target, headcount reduction, cycle-time improvement, or error-rate metric was stated. The Feb 2026 update notes Starbucks was 'still internally discussing' with 'no stated timeline', which suggests business-case prioritization remains unresolved. Without a quantified value case tied to an executive sponsor, this deal cannot progress to commercials. The highest-leverage move is to co-build a quantified ROI model with the buyer's finance or procurement operations lead, anchoring savings to supplier spend under management, contract-cycle-time reduction, or merchandise-ordering accuracy—whichever pain point the RFI scope implies.

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and primary competitor. The 23 Mar 2026 Next_Step update states 'back and forth with the outsourced team running Ariba', confirming Ariba is live and managed by a third-party team, which creates both an opportunity (dissatisfaction with outsourced support) and a risk (switching cost, data migration, re-training). The 1 May 2026 update notes 'Starbucks is still looking at swapping out', signaling the buyer has not committed to replacing Ariba but is exploring alternatives—classic status-quo drift. The RFI process itself (Jan 2026) implies a multi-vendor evaluation, but no other competitors are named in any available evidence. Ariba's entrenchment is deep: Starbucks uses it for vendor registration (Zycus had to register via Ariba to submit the RFI), and the outsourced-team model suggests a long-term services contract. The wedge is twofold: (1) Zycus offers a richer merchandise-procurement and inventory-visibility feature set (the RFI scope), which Ariba may not handle well for retail/non-food use cases, and (2) Merlin agentic AI for supplier negotiation and intake, which Ariba has no equivalent for. However, this wedge has not been activated—no demo, no workshop, no Merlin narrative in evidence. The move is to position Merlin as the Phase 2 AI differentiator and run a live use-case demo on merchandise ordering + QOH visibility to show what Ariba cannot do.

identify_pain.narrative: The buyer's pain is inferred from RFI scope and operational context but has never been explicitly quantified or owned by a named executive. The RFI (Jan 2026, per Next_Step__c) focused on 'Retail related questions for Merchandise ordering and Gross Margin / Discounts' and 'limited merchandise in eProc / Ordering and pass through data for QOH, Alternate Products, and possibly non-food merchandising', suggesting two pain points: (1) manual or fragmented procurement processes for retail merchandise (non-food items sold in Starbucks stores—mugs, tumblers, coffee beans for retail), and (2) lack of real-time inventory visibility (QOH, alternate products) to optimize ordering and reduce waste or stockouts. The 23 Mar 2026 update notes an 'outsourced team running Ariba', implying the current Ariba instance may be under-leveraged or managed by a third party, creating friction. However, no buyer quote articulates the financial or operational impact: no cost-per-transaction metric, no stockout/overstock cost, no cycle-time bottleneck, no headcount burden. The pain owner is also unclear—likely the VP of Procurement or VP of Supply Chain, neither of whom is mapped. Without a named pain owner and quantified impact, the deal cannot build urgency. The move is to co-create a pain discovery session with Ben or Scott: 'Walk us through your current merchandise-ordering process end-to-end—where does it break, and what does that cost you in time, errors, or margin leakage?'

decision_criteria.narrative: The RFI submitted 19 Jan 2026 included structured evaluation criteria, though full scoring rubric and weighting are unknown. Per Next_Step__c (Dec 2025–Jan 2026), the RFI covered 'Retail related questions for Merchandise ordering and Gross Margin / Discounts', 'limited merchandise in eProc / Ordering and pass through data for QOH [quantity on hand], Alternate Products, and possibly non-food merchandising', and required 'Reference accounts in Coffee Shop or QSR segment' plus 'Oracle On-Premise integrations'. These signal four confirmed criteria: (1) retail/merchandise procurement automation, (2) ERP integration (Oracle on-prem), (3) QSR/coffee-shop vertical references, and (4) possibly POS inventory management for non-food retail. The 1 May 2026 and 23 Mar 2026 updates note Zycus is 'weak with POS (Point of Sale) inventory management for non-food retail items', confirming POS as a decision criterion where we are exposed. No evidence that Starbucks shared RFI scoring, shortlist position, or weighted criteria post-submission. The 6-month silence suggests either (a) we did not advance, or (b) the evaluation is paused. The move is to request a debrief: 'Where did Zycus rank on your RFI scorecard, and which criteria were weighted highest?'

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the entrenched incumbent, managed by an outsourced team. Buyer is 'looking at swapping out' but has not committed. Zycus submitted RFI in Jan 2026 but has received no shortlist feedback in 6 months. Status-quo (keeping Ariba) is the path of least resistance without a compelling ROI case and executive sponsorship. Zycus has product advantage in retail-merchandise procurement and AI (Merlin), but the wedge has not been activated—no demo, no workshop, no Merlin narrative. Competitive position is weak due to incumbency inertia and lack of champion/EB to drive change.

• The buyer's stated requirements per the description are retail-related questions on merchandise ordering and gross margin/discounts, reference accounts in coffee shop or QSR, and Oracle on-premise integrations. There is zero buyer-stated mention of Merlin, Agentic, ANA, Intake or GenAI. Products include Merlin, Merlin Intake and Agentic AI, but all of that is Zycus-pushed. The seller notes the RFI is being treated more like an RFI than an RFP.
• Avoma was fully exhausted - zero calls at opportunity, account or starbucks.com domain across a multi-month RFI cycle, which is extremely unusual for a $500K deal and suggests Ariba-managed dialogue. Given the slide, the Nashville disruption and zero AI-specific buyer asks across the detailed RFI, this is Resistant rather than just cool.

gaps: No Avoma calls discovered for this opportunity via opp-id or account-level discovery (Starbucks Coffee Company, account domains: starbucks.com). All deal intelligence is derived from Salesforce Next_Step__c field (extensive rep-authored narrative covering Dec 2025–May 2026), OpportunityContactRole, and ground-truth stage/forecast/amount/dates. Without buyer calls, MEDDPICC and competitive reads are inferred from rep notes and RFI artifacts, not quoted buyer statements. This is a genuine knowledge gap that lowers confidence in pain quantification, EB identification, and competitive sentiment.

AIS (AI Score, Status, Why) fields are not populated in Salesforce for this opportunity. AI fit tier is inferred from product scope (Merlin, Agentic AI) and buyer context (enterprise retailer with complex procurement), not from a scored buyer signal. This is a hygiene gap, not a knowledge gap—AI positioning is documented in Products__c.

No field-history data for Qualified_Submission_Date__c, Formal_Eval_Submission_Date__c, or Shortlisted_Submission_Date__c; these fields may not be populated or may not exist in this org. Time-in-stage is computed from CreatedDate (22 Dec 2025) and current stage (Formal Evaluation), which is less precise than submission-date series. This is a hygiene gap affecting verdict math precision but does not prevent analysis.

Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, and Champion Contact Roles are absent. All four contact roles are mid-level (Manager, Senior Group Manager, SR Manager). MEDDPICC EB/DM/Champion reads are constructed from Next_Step narrative and role inference, not from formal Contact Role assignment or buyer-stated authority. This is a knowledge gap.

No Salesforce Tasks or Events data was retrieved (query may have returned zero or was not attempted). Task/Event history often carries competitive intel (e.g. 'follow-up after their Ariba demo'), commitments, and engagement cadence. This is a potential hygiene gap; the Next_Step__c field is unusually detailed and may have absorbed task-level content, so the knowledge gap is small.
